data.title: Weatherford: A Global Oil And Gas Service Provider With Diverse Portfolio
data.description:
Intro: An Energy Sector Stock Continually Innovating

Coming on the heels of my recent coverage of energy sector services firm Baker Hughes (BKR), and the current AI revolution driving energy demand to power data centers, in today's article I wanted to explore a 2nd firm in this niche in order to do a comparison and it is their fellow Texas-based peer, Weatherford International (NASDAQ:WFRD), a dividend payer who had its last earnings results on Feb. 5th.

According to its company website, "operators trust Weatherford to drive maximum value, streamline operations, and enhance safety."

At the proverbial cocktail party, you often get asked just what is it exactly that you do? When it comes to this company, rather than detailing each product in its vast portfolio, one thing it does that is notable and a sign of continually innovating is in the realm of oil well safety to stay ahead of potential equipment failures and wear/tear, such as its ForeSite Sense solution which "detects and measures wear to help you plan and execute corrective maintenance with minimal downtime and expense."

Because the last earnings results have already been public knowledge for several weeks now and the market has had time to react, today's article will put more weight on finding points in those earnings results and comments that may point to future upside or downside risk, more so than only talking about past results.

Thesis: Buy The Undervaluation Opportunity, Expecting More Upside To Outweigh The Negative

For my first coverage of this stock, I'll be arguing that it is a buy, agreeing with today's Wall St consensus.

I believe its upside will be driven by recent contract wins, a globally diversified portfolio, several macro factors that could drive a tailwind in this sector, being a proven cashflow generator, trading at undervaluation, and having decreased its leverage risk in the last few years.

At the same time, it is not much of a dividend stock after closer examination, is in a sector that is impacted by swings in oil/gas prices, its profit margin lags behind key peers, and its credit ratings remain just below investment grade.

Consider the following sections which go into each of these in more detail.

A Look At The Top Line: Key Contract Wins Could Drive Upside, As Well As Geo Diversity

A notable forward-viewing comment by the CEO Girish Saligram in Q4 results was the following, which I think provides at least some positive sentiment for investors to think about looking ahead:

While there is some temporary activity reduction, we continue to believe in the industry’s mid to long-term resilience and remain committed to our goal of achieving EBITDA margins in the high 20’s over the next few years.

A key factor I believe could point to future business viability and growth could be recent contract wins, since they can drive future revenue from all that new business.

For instance, some notable wins reported in Q4 results include a drilling services contract for the Kuwait Oil Company, a 5-year contract with the National Oil Company of Qatar for Weatherford to provide fishing and drilling tools, and Shell awarding a 3-year contract "to provide Well Completions and other related specialized services in onshore Nigeria."

Keep in mind, though, Weatherford has some other big peers to compete with in this space, including firms like Baker Hughes (BKR), Halliburton (HAL), and Schlumberger (SLB). Even though Weatherford has a projected positive fwd rev growth of +2.15%, both Schlumberger and Baker Hughes do beat it by a few points in this category:

One factor I look for to gauge downside risk is geographic diversification, in addition to portfolio diversification. When it comes to Weatherford and its FY24 full-year results, what I would call out is that even though the North American market saw a 2% YoY decline, the firm saw YoY revenue growth in the Europe, Africa, and Middle East regions:

I believe such broad geographic diversification will continue to reduce downside risk, particularly if the firm continues growing its market penetration in those regions outside the US where it can keep driving revenue growth in 2025, thereby not relying overly on one geography, a single client or single wonder product.

Despite Declines In OpEx, Profit Margin Could Use Improvement vs Peers

To not dwell too much on the top-line, let's talk about what could drive a healthy earnings growth in 2025, and I believe that is a strong and proven profit margin, for one, and a commitment to control costs.

However, again it is relevant to compare to peers and the sector since many investors may be picking among several similar peers to add the best one to their portfolio.

When it comes to Weatherford, although they achieved a net income margin of 9.18%, it slightly falls behind the sector average of 10.5%. The firm also falls behind its sector on gross profit margin too.

Also, since net income margin (rather than gross margin) affects the EPS directly, and a company's EPS often is what stands out in financial media headlines, grabbing investor attention, when compared to the 3 similar peers I mentioned earlier, it seems Weatherford falls behind in this metric:

On a positive note, though, looking at the OpEx trend on its income statement it appears the firm has been able to drive down its operating expenses when comparing 2018/2019 levels vs 2024:

In addition, according to its Q4 presentation, the firm reported a "340 basis points improvement in overhead costs as a % of revenue 2024 vs. 2022."

I believe this is a step in the right direction if the trend continues, and could boost that profit margin in the near term. However, since investors may also be looking at what the analyst consensus is saying one cannot ignore the projected negative YoY EPS growth of nearly -5% in FY25, indicating analysts are not overly optimistic about earnings growth in 2025.

Improvement To Leverage Risk, Though Credit Ratings Just Below Investment Grade

I want to highlight the following ychart I created which shows the debt/equity ratio of Weatherford vs 3 key peers, over the last 3 years.

While Weatherford's D/E is significantly above these peers, consider the trend shows it has declined over 3 years, which I think is a sign of improving leverage risk, a positive upside factor in my view.

From another angle, in the Q4 presentation we can see that the firm has been able to drive down its net leverage over the last few years, another positive trend:

In the current elevated interest rate environment, I would suggest to my investor readers to consider the leverage risk of a firm, as I believe this environment generally is more favorable to those carrying lower leverage.

We can see the effects of the lower debt trend on the company's income statement, as quarterly interest expense has come down 21% when comparing the quarters Dec. 2024 vs Jun 2023:

Additionally, Q4 presentation data shows another angle to the corporate debt risk story and what credit rating agencies are saying. As the firm headed into 2025, three major agencies rated it stable/positive but just below investment-grade (BB- / Ba3), as this table shows:

To help me understand what drove some of these ratings, I turned to commentary by Fitch last fall. While a positive, they mentioned was that "Weatherford's operations have significant size and are well diversified across the globe," they also pointed out that this "industry remains highly cyclical with issuers often being the first in the energy space to feel the negative impacts of commodity price declines."

My impression in this case is that the lower leverage position should be a positive for investors to consider, while the credit ratings still have some way to go in order to reach investment grade and that could be a negative to consider.

Several Macro Factors To Consider Which Could Add More Upside And Downside

Taking a step back from what we discussed so far, let's look at the bigger market picture in this space and where Weatherford may or may not have a market edge looking ahead, to add another angle to this discussion.

Notable to mention is that Yahoo! Finance reported today that new data from research firm Introspective Market Research indicates Weatherford could be one of the firms to get tailwind from market growth forecasts in the niche of oilfield drilling elevators.

According to the article, "the Oilfield Drilling Elevator Market is vital for efficient drilling operations, handling heavy tubular goods," and this niche is expected to see "a strong CAGR of 2.78% from 2024 to 2032."

Further, as I mentioned before that the artificial intelligence/AI demand trend could drive a huge tailwind to electricity power demand, which is relevant to the oil/gas sector in terms of gas-powered electric plants.

In fact, last May Goldman Sachs Research "estimates that data center power demand will grow 160% by 2030."

We also have a new federal government in the US, and one that has not only hinted at a policy of allowing more drilling to occur (more production) but a BBC article just last week has pointed out that some other countries may follow the lead:

In response to Trump's 'drill, baby, drill' slogan aimed at ramping up fossil fuel extraction, and the US notifying the UN of its withdrawal from the Paris climate agreement, Indonesia, for instance, has hinted that it may follow suit.

I believe the above factors, such as increased AI-driven power demand, the expected growth in the oilfield drilling elevator market, and the new public policy encouraging more production, could drive tailwind to the niche sector that Weatherford is in.

At the same time, the larger sector is affected by cyclical trends like oil and gas prices since they can affect the profitability of launching and running oil wells, which could impact the need for oil well services too.

From what I gathered among official data sources like the US Energy Information Administration in February, they "forecast that the Brent crude oil price will average $74 per barrel in 2025 before falling to $66/b in 2026," citing the push/pull between expected OPEC cuts to supply which could boost or support prices but also considering expected gradual increases in production.

A Low-Yield Dividend That Lacks Proven Growth, But Strong Cashflow Could Sustain It

This stock is being included in my dividend ideas this month, so let's consider it from the perspective of a dividend investor.

Unfortunately, at a yield of under 2% it is not ideal for high-yield portfolios if that is someone's goal, but it also does not have a proven dividend growth model yet so it does not make the case as a dividend grower either.

What is a positive in this case is the positive free cash flow in which the firm has proven itself over several quarters, and I believe this helps the case that the firm is a steady cashflow generator, who has much lower debt now than before, and therefore more cash to commit to dividend payments.

In addition, I would point out the dividend payout ratio being just a mere 7.4%, allowing so much wiggle room and reducing the risk of a dividend cut.

Trading Below Average And Undervalued After Weak Q4 Results

At this point, we have said a lot about various factors to consider this stock, so in this section it is time to consider where the market is pricing it.

In the chart I created below what sticks out about this stock is that it is trading 32% below its 200-day simple moving average, somewhere in the middle of its 5 year high/low:

Right off the bat, I suspect the Q4 results also did not help boost the share price, considering the firm's Q4 revenue, gross profit, and net income all saw YoY declines on the income statement.

Also, the market is valuating this stock at a forward earnings multiple of just 9.7x, a few points below the sector average of 12.3x.

It also is undervalued to its sector in terms of fwd EV/EBITDA.

Further, peer data shows this stock as undervalued when comparing the fwd earnings multiple (GAAP) vs 2 of its 3 key peers:

The trading price well below its moving average, along with the undervaluation metrics, should give some upside points to this stock since I believe it could attract value-motivated investors who look for a profitable company that happens to be cheap.

However, to decide whether it is valuable and cheap or just cheap, please read my overall conclusion next:

Conclusion: Agree With Bullish Wall St Rating

To conclude this thesis, the following rating table summarizes all of the upside and downside factors I went over today, and the weighted rating score I gave each:

Based on my findings, there is much more upside potential than downside risk, and therefore I am calling this stock a buy for my very first coverage of it.

I gave greater weight to upside factors like the major contract wins, its proven cashflow generation, and trading so far below its moving average, but also gave extra weight to downside factors like the cyclical impact of oil and gas prices to this sector. Compared to consensus ratings, my rating is closest to the Wall St consensus today:

Wrap Up: A Look At Potential Challenges To My Thesis

To wrap up, I would mention that some factors that could negatively impact my bullish call is a drastic drop in oil and gas prices in the next two years far more than forecasted, but also a vast drop in new oil wells and production since this is a servicing company that benefits when new oil wells are launched.

The longer-term outcome of the AI revolution and its subsequent power demands in the next decades may also turn out to be overly hyped up like the 2000 dotcom bubble and therefore overly betting solely on AI could present some portfolio risk, as I believe companies will increasingly try to justify a positive ROI from their AI investments.

As this article is part of my ongoing theme on this portal called Financial Markets with Albert Anthony, where I try to find stocks to use in growing a dividend income portfolio, my question for readers today is do you think the AI story is overinflated or still has lots of upside potential?

data.title: CEF Weekly Review: Western Asset Follows The Nuveen Term CEF Playbook
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the third week of February. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

CEFs were mixed on the week as stocks fell and Treasuries rose. Disappointing Walmart numbers and a jump in consumer inflation expectations hit stocks and pushed interest rates lower.

CEF discounts have been tightening this year with fixed-income sectors trading tighter on average than their equity counterparts.

Market Themes

The Western Asset High Yield Defined Opportunity Fund (HYI) is following the usual Nuveen playbook for term CEFs. HYI will ask shareholders to approve a change into a perpetual fund. If this is approved, it will hold a tender offer of 100% shares at the NAV. If there are enough assets (>$75m) remaining in the fund, it will do the tender offer and turn into a perpetual fund. If there are not enough assets in the fund, it will not do the tender offer and just terminate.

The best outcome for investors would be for HYI to remain outstanding (the more funds the better) and to get out at the NAV (fully monetizing the discount). This means that investors would want to vote yes on the proposal and then to tender shares.

Obviously, if everyone does this then there would be fewer than $75m of assets remaining, the tender offer will be cancelled and the fund will terminate. However, interestingly, that often doesn’t happen. For example, JPI remained outstanding and turned into a perpetual fund.

The risk to investors who don’t tender is that the discount tends to widen out for perpetual funds. Given HYI discount is now not far off zero, the risk is that when it turns into a perpetual CEF, the discount will widen. An example of what happened to JPI is shown below.

The median discount in the sector is 3% and would likely be wider for HYI given its lower distribution rate. The HYI tender offer is expected to happen in Q3, closer to its planned termination date of 30-Sep.

Market Commentary

The BlackRock Muni CEF MUI was delisted. It is in the process of converting from an exchange-listed CEF to an unlisted CEF. The fund will conduct quarterly repurchases and will be named the BlackRock Municipal Credit Alpha Portfolio. The conversion will take place on 24-March, with the initial repurchase offer commencing in the second quarter.

MUI was highlighted here at the time of its tender offer in November as a way to get in on a slow-burn interval fund, not dissimilar to the periodic tender-offer public funds like EIM and MVF. Prior to the delisting it was trading at a double-digit discount, as was expected given the majority of investors aren’t keen on being in an unlisted asset. Interestingly, someone liked it enough that it rallied 3% on its last day as a public fund. It will take time to monetize the wide discount by selling the fund however the alpha will keep ticking along for holders.

Two Gabelli CEFs GAB and GGT have reaffirmed their managed distribution policies. The actual policy is somewhat unusual in that it calls for the highest of the minimum annual distribution of 10% of the average NAV within a calendar year and the IRS guidelines for RICs (i.e. to distribute at least 90% of taxable income and distribute at least 98.2% of ordinary income and capital gain income to avoid excise tax).

Typically, CEFs have either a fixed distribution policy or a floating one where the distribution is a function of the NAV with some look back. The GAB distribution is 10.8% of the NAV and the GGT distribution is 22.7% of the NAV. GGT has struggled with a total NAV return of around 3% CAGR over the last 5 years. GAB has done better with an 11% total NAV return.

Interestingly the premium of GGT is higher than that of GAB which tells us that the market thinks distributions are more important than historic performance for valuation purposes. Of course, this allows more discerning investors to take advantage of these mispricings and generate alpha over time.

data.title: Olam Group Limited 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation

data.title: The Fed Continues With QT, And Winds Down Emergency Measures
data.description:
The following analysis breaks down the Fed balance sheet in detail. It shows different parts of the balance sheet and how those amounts have changed. It also shows historical interest rate trends.

Breaking Down the Balance Sheet

Back in May 2024, the Fed announced a reduction in QT from $95B to $60B. The data shows that this was moving forward as expected, mostly coming in under the target amount. In November, there was a big run-off, and since then, the Fed has allowed $19B, $68B and $52B to roll off the balance sheet. All combined, the run-off over the last 4 months has averaged about the $60B target.

Figure: 1 Monthly Change by Instrument

The table below provides more detail on the Fed’s QT efforts.

Figure: 2 Balance Sheet Breakdown

The weekly activity can be seen below. As shown, it’s been mostly consistent with big chunks along the way. It is also allowing a range of maturities and products to roll off.

Figure: 3 Fed Balance Sheet Weekly Changes

The chart below shows the balance on detailed items in Loans and also Repos. These were the programs set up in the wake of the SVB collapse early last year. All of the programs have dropped down to zero at this point showing that the emergency measures have come to a close.

Figure: 4 Loan Details

Yields

Yields have been fluctuating within a band since Sept 2022, ranging mostly between 3.25% and 4.75%.

Figure: 5 Interest Rates Across Maturities

The spread between the 2-year and 10-year has finally turned positive. When the inverted yield curve predicts recession, the recession does not usually occur until sometime after the spread turns positive. This ranges between 6 and 18 months. Considering we are hitting the 6-month point now, this could be a bad sign for the months ahead.

Figure: 6 Tracking Yield Curve Inversion

The chart below shows the current yield curve, the yield curve one month ago, and one year ago. It is clear to see the short-end coming down while the long-end has remained mostly flat.

Figure: 7 Tracking Yield Curve Inversion

The Fed Takes Losses

When the Fed makes money, it sends it back to the Treasury. This has netted the Treasury close to $100B a year. This can be seen below.

Figure: 8 Fed Payments to Treasury

You may notice in the chart above that 2023 and 2024 are showing $0. That’s because the Fed has been losing money. According to the Fed: The Federal Reserve Banks remit residual net earnings to the U.S. Treasury after providing for the costs of operations… Positive amounts represent the estimated weekly remittances due to U.S. Treasury. Negative amounts represent the cumulative deferred asset position… deferred asset is the amount of net earnings that the Federal Reserve Banks need to realize before remittances to the U.S. Treasury resume.

Basically, when the Fed makes money, it gives it to the Treasury. When it loses money, it keeps a negative balance by printing the difference. That negative balance has just exceeded $224B! This negative balance is increasing by about $10B a month!

Figure: 9 Remittances or Negative Balance

Who Will Fill the Gap?

The Fed has not been buying in the Treasury market for over a year (they have been selling); however, the Treasury is still issuing tons of new debt. Who has been picking up the slack since the Fed stepped away?

International holdings have increased a decent amount over the last year by over $400B. Unfortunately, this pales in comparison to the amount of debt issued by the Treasury overall which is closer to $2T.

Note: data is updated on a lag. The latest data is as of December

Figure: 10 International Holders

It should be noted that China continues to reduce holdings of US Treasuries. They have dropped $500B over the last decade, while Japan has been mostly flat over the same time. The slack has been picked up by other countries.

Figure: 11 Average Weekly Change in the Balance Sheet

Historical Perspective

The final plot below takes a larger view of the balance sheet. It is clear to see how the usage of the balance sheet has changed since the Global Financial Crisis.

Figure: 12 Historical Fed Balance Sheet

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FIBRA Prologis Is Twice As Large Post Terrafina Acquisition But Still Expensive
data.description:
FIBRA Prologis (OTCPK:FBBPF), FPrologis for short, released 4Q24 earnings, the first financial statements with the combined interest in FIBRA Terrafina (OTCPK:CBAOF) owned at 90%.

In terms of strategy, the company is moving forward to sell about 5% of its total assets this year (a net of 2% after purchases), mainly from Terrafina's non-core portfolio. Management noted some pockets of uncertainty in the market coming from tariffs, but is confident that these will be resolved.

The combined entity generated about $270 million in FFO, which represents only a 5.5% yield on a current market cap of $4.9 billion. The yield on next year's guided FFO is 6.5%. I maintain that the company is not attractive at these prices, especially considering the uncertainty and the position in the Mexican industrial real estate cycle. I maintain my Hold for FIBRA Prologis.

4Q24 results

Combined operations: The 4Q24 report contains the first combined financial statements for the new FPrologis (a combination of the previous FPrologis and Terrafina). The company has more than doubled its gross leasable area and is now operating in more cities thanks to the addition of the Terrafina portfolio.

The combined companies generated about $270 million in FFO, at a 65% margin on operating revenues ($412 million, before property appreciation accruals), over an equity base of $7 billion (or 3.8% net return on assets).

Higher leverage: The company has added significant debt, now totaling about $2.25 billion (from about $750 million in FY23), in order to pay for Terrafina's acquisition.

Although debt is a very small part of equity (approximately 1/3), and the effective rate is not high (4.5% to 6%), it does represent almost 10 years of FFO and 1/3 of FFO ex-interest.

Further, the company has added at least $500 million more in maturities between 2025 and 2027. Management is confident this will be paid off as part of Terrafina's portfolio is sold.

The process of divestment started: FPrologis wants to operate only in six cities of Mexico (CDMX, Guadalajara, Monterrey, Reynosa, Juarez, and Tijuana). This means a part of Terrafina's portfolio ($1.5 billion as commented on the earnings call, as much as 50% of Terrafina's portfolio as reported in the earnings report) is expected to be sold. FY25 guidance indicated $400 million in sales, or less than 1/3. The rest of the sales and the price received will be dependent on market conditions.

Market commentary: Outside of the combination, the market commentary is the most important aspect of the release.

Management recognized that the uncertainty generated by the potential tariffs from the US to Mexico or the renegotiation of the free trade agreements between the two countries is delaying investment in the north of the country.

Management said that in their opinion, the uncertainty is not generating any closures, downsizing, or even delays in renewals. However, it is generating delays in expansion by present or new companies.

Price risk in 2025: About 20% of the combined entity's portfolio is going for renewal in 2025 (no disclosure about how much of this is going to be sold). The current price per square fit is $5.92 on this portion of the portfolio, and renewal prices could be as much as 77% higher (61% higher for the FY24 renewals). The risk here is that given market uncertainty, and potentially higher vacancies, customers ask for lower prices, and the company cannot lock better terms on these leases.

Valuation is high on earnings

FPrologis expects to generate about $0.22 in FFO per ADR in 2025, or a 6.5% yield on the current ~$3.18 price.

In my opinion, this is very low.

The main reason is that it is based on super-low vacancies of about 3% versus a median of 6%+ between 2013-2019, that is, the 6.5% yield is a cycle-top yield.

In addition, the Mexican industrial real estate market is highly uncertain at this point, which could easily make future lease price projections unachievable, or even lead to much higher vacancies.

In my opinion, considering the uncertainty of the situation, and the position in the cycle, the company should trade at a much higher yield on FFO (or conversely lower multiple on FFO).

Some might point that the company trades at 60% of book value, but I would point out that this book value is based on appraisals carried at fair value on the balance sheet, and therefore sensible to inputs determined by the cycle, and to the cap rate applied for funds expected. Both can move negatively during a down cycle, closing the valuation gap if things are not so positive in Mexico's industrial real estate.

For this reason, I prefer to maintain a Hold on the new FIBRA Prologis.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Broadcom: Must Hold Until Correction Stops
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) is a firm that has been in an uptrend since 2009. Even though sometimes it’s getting into accumulation phases, we’ve never seen it get into a downtrend in the long term. In this article, I will focus on its place in the developing AI and tech sector and I will explain why my position is “hold”.

AVGO’s Place in the Tech Sector

As every AVGO investor knows, the company was producing chips for years. But only since 2023-2024 they started to provide AI chips to gigantic firms like OpenAI, Apple (AAPL), Google (GOOGL) and Meta (META).

The company made its entrance to AI sector with Trident 5-X12 Chip which they produced in November 2023. That chip included the world’s first on-chip neural network inference engine called NetGNT and designed for data centers. After that, the company started to develop custom AI chips for big tech companies. Until today, AVGO developed AI chips for Google Cloud service and OpenAI.

While I’m talking about the place of AVGO in the AI sector, since their area of expertise is not technology, investors and financial analysts may confuse AVGO's chips with NVDA’s or make the mistake of comparing them. But there’s a big difference about chips structures, application range and customer base. NVDA develops graphic processing units (GPU) while AVGO develops application specific integrated circuits (ASIC). NVDA has more versatile solutions while AVGO highly specialized for AI tasks.

I want to talk about AI chips that AVGO actively producing. Company announced 3.5D XDSiP Technology in January 2025 which designed for AI accelerators. In my opinion as AI sector grows this chip is going to find a big demand. In addition to that you’ve most probably heard Apple Intelligence which AAPL is using in their newest iPhones. AVGO right now collaborating with AAPL on developing “Baltra” chip which is expected to be ready for mass production in 2026. This chip is going to be used in AAPL’s AI services.

After talking about chips and solutions I want to take your attention to the segments of the revenue. Revenue is generated by two segments which are semiconductor solutions and infrastructure software. In the 4th quarter semiconductor solutions generates more than half of the revenue but there’s more important point in this situation. Even though infrastructure software creates less than half of the revenue when I look at the YoY growth of this segment I see that segment grew 196% which is remarkable.

This growth occurred because of several factors. First of all AVGO’s VMware integration affected this growth very much since this provided strong revenue stream from software licensing. Plus AVGO shifted toward subscription based model for these VMware integration really affected well.

Do I think this segment would grow more? Since there’s increasing need for virtualization and AI solutions I really think AVGO’s offerings are big supply for these demands. Plus VMware’s customer base giving AVGO really big opportunity to expand and access to larger enterprises and agencies. That’s why I think this segment will continue to grow. In addition to that semiconductor segment will continue to grow naturally since they’re already doing many partnerships and collaborations with big companies as I mentioned before.

I would like to point out that AVGO has very advantageous opportunities in the AI semiconductor market too. President Trump’s team is seeking to strengthen the chip controls over China and I believe they’re going to invest more and more in domestic semiconductor manufacturing across U.S. These geopolitical developments gives me positive feeling about AVGO’s place in the semiconductor and AI sectors.

I'm not the only one thinking that. Company states that they expect that their AI revenue is going to reach to the range of $60-90 billion in the fiscal year of 2027. And when I analyze all qualitative improvements of the company, I don’t see any obstacle in front of this target.

Financials

Revenue

After qualitative developments, I believe it’s important to analyze financial numbers of the company. First of all, when I look at the quarterly revenue since 2022, especially after the last quarter of 2023, we’re seeing a big surge. Until the 2023, revenue was roaming between $7-10 billion, but after the end of 2023, revenue jumped nearly to $12 billion. As I told in the earlier paragraphs, this surge caused by the VMware acquisition and AI semiconductor growth.

When I apply linear regression calculation on the revenue since 2022, I see a significant uptrend which is really positive for a stock. In this case, I want to take the median of revenue’s QoQ change rates since 2022. Which gives me the result of 4.68%. This median is pretty consistent with the qualitative developments of AVGO. That’s why I forecast that Q1 2025 revenue of AVGO is going to be around $14.7 billion by increasing QoQ 4.68%.

Net Income

AVGO’s net income is pretty consistent until the last quarter of 2023. Then what happened and revenue increased in the last quarter of 2023 but net income decreased? The VMware acquisition had a significant effect on amortization expenses related to intangible assets. These non-cash charges were heavily weighed on net income. Plus in the second quarter of 2024 AVGO recorded minus net income which looked very bad in that quarter. But this loss was primarily due to one time discrete non-cash tax provision of $4.5 billion which is related to transfer of intellectual property rights to the U.S.

Except these extraordinary and one time events AVGO’s net income median is $3.188 billion and in the last quarter of 2024 we’ve seen that AVGO’s net income rose over that. Since everything turned normal, and we don’t have these extraordinary events, I expect net income to stay over this median level.

Valuation

Forward P/E

I want to start evaluating AVGO and its competitors by looking at their Forward P/E ratios. First I chose AVGO's competitors as TSM, TXN, QCOM and AMD. When I look at the trend of forward P/E ratio of AVGO since 2023, I see that it's in an uptrend until July 2024. Since then, AVGO's forward P/E is between 40-25x. When I look at the whole sector wide I see that they were all in uptrend until July 2024, which gives me an idea that AVGO is not diverging from the sector.

Normally, even 5x P/E shows us that stocks are overvalued in some sectors, but in the semiconductor sector I calculate an average of 22.85x. So right now I can tell that AVGO is above this average.

Forward P/S

When I look at the forward P/S ratio, I see that AVGO's valuation is worse than P/E. Since 1 year AVGO's P/S is increasing significantly which tells us stock is getting more expensive to buy every day. Sector's most recent forward P/S average is 7.04x and AVGO's P/S ratio is 15.80x, which makes it more than 2 times expensive compared to the sector average.

Yes, qualitative developments and financials are good and getting better every quarter, but valuations keeping me to give a "buy" position. In my opinion, there's going to be definitely better times to buy AVGO than these ratios.

Charting

When I look at the chart of AVGO since 2022, I see a significant uptrend, especially after the beginning of 2023. To correctly identify this uptrend, I’m using Hodrick-Prescott filter, which I trust very much in this type of time frame.

AVGO is above HP filter since 2023, and it tested this filter many times as support level in the second half of 2024. After the last price surge until $250 in the last quarter of 2024 AVGO started a correction. I believe this correction is going to last at least until $187 level, which is HP filter that it found support many times before.

Since the beginning of 2025 AVGO’s momentum is decreasing and right now, MACD indicator shows us there’s a negative momentum which can be last at least 2 more weeks. My forecast is based on past movements of MACD. For example, it can be seen that AVGO many times corrected to HP filter in 2024 and these corrections reflected as a couple weeks negative momentum to MACD indicator.

In this case, I think AVGO can find a support at $187 level and continue its run. In the long term, when I calculate the yearly expected return of AVGO I find 37.81% which is actually very good. That means that valuations shows that stock is expensive and charting shows that correction is going to continue, but in the long term AVGO is not a bad stock to keep. That’s why I keep my “hold” position instead of “buy” or “sell”.

Risk

The conclusion of my analysis pointing out that even though AVGO is overvalued to buy right now it's very good investment in the medium and long term. Especially in 2025 there are many risks that AVGO can encounter, and these risks can pull its price down below $187.

With the new government of U.S. there's an ongoing U.S.-China trade tensions pose many risks on semiconductor firms like AVGO. Since these tensions and events are focused on the semiconductor sector itself, for example, any disruption about sourcing components from China can affect AVGO's production line.

I talked about AVGO's growing place in the developing AI sector. But at the same time, other areas like broadband and industrial markets that AVGO is working are showing softness. For example, AVGO's broadband revenue declined by 51% YoY in Q4 2024. If these segments fail to recover they can weigh on the revenue growth.

Conclusion

AVGO is a semiconductor company that are developing themselves continuously day by day. Especially the moves that they are doing to get a place in the growing AI sector benefits their revenue very much. Their revenue is growing and financials are turning stable again after one time costs in the 3rd quarter 2024. I see these as positive indicators for AVGO but at the same time valuations are showing that the company is much more expensive to buy than it's peers in the sector. Plus, technical indicators and charting analysis shows that AVGO is going to continue its correction until $187. That's why as a conclusion, my position for AVGO is "hold".

data.title: KORU: Navigating 2025 Volaility
data.description:
Overview

South Korean equities have been under heavy pressure since 2024 due to the combination of weaker export growth and new political risks in December. These factors have caused South Korea to massively underperform emerging market equities since Q4 2024.

However, Korean equities have bounced back in January, displaying that much of the political and economic concerns are more likely short term in nature. South Korea also trades at a massive discount to emerging markets, despite its superior export structure and heavier weighting in the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

The upcoming trade war and broader emerging market risks will create a lot of pullbacks and strong entry points for South Korea. South Korea looks like a strong long term hold, but it could also be a good market to trade if there are any selloffs later this year. Trading leveraged ETFs in these situations can be appealing, typically if the holding period is less than a month.

The Direxion Daily MSCI South Korea Bull 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:KORU) has rallied strongly this year, following a strong decline in December.

This ETF has already had a strong YTD return following some of the political tensions in December, and this month doesn't look like a good entry point. However, this looks like an acceptable vehicle for quick event-driven trades in the future.

ETF Overview

The Direxion Daily MSCI South Korea Bull 3X Shares (KORU) provides leveraged exposure to South Korea’s stock market. Its goal to deliver daily investment of 300% of the MSCI Korea 25/50 Index’s performance. This daily return is calculated based on the return after fees and expenses have been paid. The net expense ratio for this ETF is 1.34%.

This ETF can be great during bull runs, providing returns that may even exceed 3x the returns of the MSCI Korea Index. However, declines can also compound, and this can be a disastrous vehicle if it is held for months or even weeks.

As seen below, things can turn sour even during one quarter. The combination of sluggish economic growth and political risks in December caused this ETF to decline by nearly 50% during Q4 2024. Investors who held this ETF during this time period would now need this ETF to nearly double in 2025 to recover.

However, investors who used this vehicle to trade the week after South Korea's government declared martial law in December, would have been able to score a quick 23% return in just two months.

These examples are displayed as a cautionary tale. If you lose on one of these short-term bets, it's better to get out as fast as possible, as the multi-month drawdowns can easily meet or exceed 50%.

Recent Trends

South Korea's stock market easily bounced back from the December lows but has been hesitant to enter a full bull run. Korean equities outperformed MSCI Emerging Markets in January and still trade at a substantial discount to MSCI Emerging Markets. Equities may even trade at a single-digit PE ratio this year if the market punishes South Korea because of trade war tensions or political risks. This low valuation looks like a solid setup for both long-term investors and short-term traders.

The market is still likely concerned about the economic outlook, which is a bit more sluggish, and the continued political risks. However, February has also been a strong month for Korean equities, which allowed this ETF to strongly break out relative to its benchmark.

Most of the post December gains have likely been made for the market, and while the valuation for South Korea looks stellar, there is still likely a stronger chance of short-term pullbacks in the market amid the soaring global tensions and weaker South Korean economic outlook this year.

South Korea Outlook

South Korea's economic growth will be a bit slower this year, as volatility from the semiconductor industry and other electronics industries has weighed on its export growth. Growth has begun to approach recession levels again, but Korea's economy has been very resilient and bounced back in line with the recovery of the semiconductor industry.

South Korea Quarterly GDP Growth

Slower Growth: South Korea’s economy will likely only grow by around 1.6% this year, compared to 2% in 2024. However, the market already seems priced for this relative decline in its growth rate, and there could still be a lot of upside surprises in 2025 due to South Korea's comparatively stronger relationship with the United States and its export structure.

The country's Central Bank has noted that it needed to cut interest rates amid fears of the impact of tariffs on the economy. South Korea has previously been a victor in electronics exports, coming out ahead after the brief slump in the industry in 2023. However, the current threat of across the board tariffs has made its growth outlook hazy, and the country is currently boxed in this electronics/tech export niche, which could be a prime target. While political risks in December caused a strong sell off, the market has bounced back from this event, and concerns over export growth/trade war risks seem more prominent now.

Moving forward, South Korea's equity market performance will most likely trade in line with the performance of its tech exports. These are a key driver of its economic growth, and the index this ETF tracks has a very high weighting in information technology.

These leveraged ETFs can do extremely well when unloved markets bounce back. For example, the similarly leveraged Direxion Daily FTSE China Bull 3X Shares (YINN) has returned over 130% in the past year following the rally last year. There are some exceptions where a leveraged ETF can be held for a year or longer, but these events are not that common. Moreover, most of the gains with this ETF were made during the first few months.

This ETF may move in line with the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares (SOXL), which has also declined around 40% in the past six months. South Korea has still been much more volatile during the past six months.

Other Korea Options: Other South Korea focused ETFs and closed end funds have sold off strongly in the past year. The Korea Fund may deliver a bit more upside during the next bull run since it trades at a 15% discount, but so far it has underperformed the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF.

Time Decay: One final important factor to note is that all of these ETFs have an inevitable time decay element. Bullish and bearish leveraged ETFs will deliver the same results in the long run. At best, it's only a good idea to trade this ETF after a sell off, and to get out of the trade in a couple of weeks if it doesn't bounce back.

These ETFs only track these returns based on the daily performance. The example charts I showed display how trading these ETFs over weeks or even months can sometimes work, but anything over a year is a definite no. Bearish and bullish leveraged ETFs have the same fate in the long run.

If there are any sell-offs in the South Korean market, caused by tensions over tariffs, political risks, or its economic slowdown, this would be a good trading vehicle to check out after equities bottom out. South Korea's stock market already trades at 8.4x forward earnings and slightly below book value, so a further decline isn't rational at the moment, even with growth coming in slower in 2025. While equities and traditional ETFs are a much safer bet, this vehicle looks like a good one to watch in 2025.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - Tariffs, NFP And ECB Meeting On The Agenda
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in Review: Tariff Chatter Continues to Drive Market Moves

Markets have been through another week where tariffs have been the driving force behind market moves. The PCE data, which I had thought would be the major event for the week, failed to stoke any real volatility, and that is testament to what we have seen in 2025 thus far.

Markets are more occupied with the moves of the Trump administration than they are about the data.

US data this week showed signs that the economy may be stalling, while overall market sentiment has been dented by tariff chatter and inflationary concerns. This was reflected in the CB consumer confidence data, which showed a significant uptick in the 12-month interest rate expectations by consumers.

U.S. consumer spending dropped in January for the first time in almost two years, and the goods trade gap hit a record high as businesses sped up imports to avoid tariffs. This points to weak economic growth or even a possible contraction this quarter.

Data from the Commerce Department also showed that while inflation slowed down yearly, prices remain stubbornly high with steady monthly increases. Meanwhile, the Trump administration is increasing tariffs, which economists warn will lead to higher prices as businesses pass costs to consumers.

Inflation expectations among consumers rose sharply in February. The Atlanta Federal Reserve cut its first quarter GDP forecast, now predicting the economy will shrink by 1.5% annually instead of growing at the earlier estimate of 2.3%.

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 struggles continued this week, which is no surprise given the current market dynamics. The Fear and Greed index is now in fear territory as tariff chatter ramps up, and economic data continues to underwhelm.

On the FX front, the Dollar returned to its king status this week, weighing on US dollar denominated pairs, with the Euro losing ground and retreating from the psychological 1.0500 handle.

Gold is the biggest loser this week, and I was taken aback by the selloff. Mostly down to the fact that tariffs have until now been a positive for safe haven demand. The only explanation I can see is potential profit taking and gold traders concerned by the rise in inflation expectations.

Oil prices also struggled this week on growing concerns about global growth and Oil demand. A disagreement by OPEC + members on an output increase from April provided a brief respite on Thursday but was followed by another bearish day to end the week.

Crypto markets are also feeling the pinch, with Bitcoin on course for its biggest weekly drop since the collapse of FTX in 2022.

The Week Ahead: Tariffs at the Forefront. Will Trump Follow Through?

Asia Pacific Markets

The main focus this week in the Asia Pacific region for me is China's Two Sessions meeting.

Markets are closely watching China's Government Work Report, which Premier Li Qiang will deliver on March 5. It will reveal the country's 2025 growth target and outline key policies. China is expected to keep its growth goal at "around 5.0%," the same as in 2024, while giving more details about fiscal and monetary plans.

This Saturday morning, China will release its official February purchasing managers' index (PMI), with a slight growth at 50.1 expected. The Caixin PMI will follow on Monday. On Friday, China Customs will provide the first trade data for the first two months of 2025.

Europe + UK + US

In developed markets, the US PCE data released last week failed to shake markets as expected, but the Trump administration continues to throw curveballs for Global Market participants to navigate.

Next week is important for the US, with trade tariffs on imports from China, Mexico, and Canada possibly starting on March 4, along with key economic data, like the February jobs report.

President Trump argues that foreign countries will bear the cost due to a stronger dollar, but consumer prices may rise, similar to past tariffs. Consumer confidence is already shaky due to spending concerns and austerity measures, and more tariff news could worsen the outlook and dent overall market sentiment.

Economic data, including the ISM reports, is expected to show slow growth. Manufacturing and services surveys suggest a weaker outlook, and corporate caution over changing trade policies may lead to only a small rise in payrolls. Federal job cuts may take months to show a clear impact.

Next week, the European Central Bank will decide on interest rates after reviewing a new inflation report. A 0.25% rate cut is expected, though inflation might rise in February before easing. With more hawkish voices in the ECB, debates on the final rate for the eurozone are growing more important.

Unemployment is also in focus. Despite businesses cutting jobs recently, the unemployment rate remains at a record low of 6.3%. Any shift in the labor market could impact how far the ECB can lower rates.

Chart of the Week

This week's focus is on the US Dollar Index (DXY) after a solid recovery this week which has propelled the DXY back above the 107.00 handle.

A lot of the move was down to the tariff chatter which arose toward the backend of the week, and that is likely to carry over into next week.

Currently, the DXY is supported by the 107.00 handle, with the 100-day MA resting at 106.700 also likely to provide support.

Immediate resistance rests at 108.00 before the 108.49 handle comes into focus.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart - February 28, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 107.00
  • 106.13
  • 105.63

Resistance

  • 108.00
  • 108.49
  • 109.52

Original Post

data.title: EWQ Struggles In A Shaky French Economy
data.description:
Things aren't looking so good for the French economy, which is also weighing on French equities.

The iShares MSCI France ETF (NYSEARCA:EWQ), which tracks the main mid-to-large size companies in France, has performed poorly over the last twelve months, at levels similar to the devaluation of the Euro versus the U.S. Dollar, reflecting a series of structural and cyclical problems marked by significant challenges, with high levels of indebtedness, a growing fiscal deficit and a tense political environment.

The GDP growth has been modest, with the economy struggling to boost production and consumption, the debt/GDP ratio well above that recommended by the EU, and a fiscal deficit that already exceeds 6% of GDP.

Specifically for EWQ, this has been a global ETF with above-average volatility and poor historical annual returns. In the current scenario, even though some of its main holdings have exposure to the global economy and the Eurozone, rather than the French one specifically, the ETF still trades at a valuation above the main Eurozone equities and has few or no short- to medium-term catalysts for a turnaround.

As a result, I see EWQ more likely to underperform over the course of 2025.

An Overview of France's Economy

The French economy is at a crossroads that many predicted but hoped would never come. At first glance, you might notice a high GDP, a robust business sector, and a long history of global influence in Europe and beyond.

The point to note is that below this surface, there are political risks and out-of-control debt levels heading upwards. Recent events since President Emmanuel Macron took the decision to dissolve parliament in June have brought the Macron government close to collapse, not only affecting France's political instability but also reflecting on the Eurozone.

Looking ahead, at the end of the day, the question that remains unanswered is how France gets to this stage and how it can avoid the dangerous spiral that some of the leading indicators are showing today.

Debt Levels and Debt Growth

Arguably, debt acts in a central way during economic cycles. When debt accumulates, there is short-term growth, but if it grows too quickly, it can lead to financial instability. That said, the main point is how debt grows in relation to the economy, as measured by the debt-to-GDP ratio, or how debt growth will be sustained in the long term.

Currently, France is dealing with a debt estimated at more than €3 trillion. Its debt-to-GDP ratio is around 112%, which far exceeds the limits recommended by the European Union.

Although some countries have also flouted these guidelines, France stands out due to its historically large public sector, its inability to contain deficits over the years, and its intense political divisions. In more stable times, this challenge might have been manageable, but when the government itself is on the brink of collapse, the slightest nudge can trigger economic anxieties across Europe.

Let's see in the graph below how since the pandemic, France has no longer managed to return to levels below 100% in the debt/GDP ratio, and although on a downward curve since 2020, in 2024 there was an increase of 111% versus 110% in 2023.

In recent months, French parliamentarians have been grappling with how to pass a budget that tackles the growing deficits. France is facing a genuine budget crisis, especially when considering the possibility of new global trade disputes or tariffs, especially if US policy changes direction. France thus risks sliding into a situation reminiscent of the Eurozone crisis that rocked the continent some 15 years ago.

In the worst-case scenario, this would send shockwaves far beyond France's borders.

At the center of the current storm is a struggle in the National Assembly over how to move forward with major austerity fiscal measures.

Some policymakers argue that austerity is crucial to restoring fiscal discipline, pointing out that the country's deficit which closed at 5.2% of GDP is now above 6% of GDP—two times higher than the already lenient EU allowances.

Others counter that people are still dealing with the consequences of the pandemic and shouldn't be burdened with sudden pension reforms or cuts in health and social spending.

There are also proposals to increase taxes on large corporations or reverse recent corporate tax cuts, but this alone cannot solve the problem if government spending remains huge.

Productivity Growth

It's not just the deficit that alarms observers, but also the structural nature of the French economy.

France has long been noted for its high labor productivity. However the French work fewer hours due to the 35-hour working week, generous annual leave, and a cultural emphasis on quality of life. This can cause gross GDP figures to appear lower than they would if workers put in more hours. High productivity is usually a sign of a healthy economy, but it can mask other deep concerns.

When we look at the economic output growth of the French economy, i.e. GDP growth, we can see timid but steady growth from 2022 onwards. However, look at how in the fourth quarter of 2024, after ten consecutive quarters of growth, there was a contraction, of 0.1%. This contraction, according to ING, is partly due to the aftermath of the Olympic Games, but political uncertainty and a less favorable international environment also played a role.

The big turning point here is that, although France's gross debt has been contracting since the height of COVID-19, the projection indicates a resumption of growth for the next three years, which would be a growth of ~124% from 2016 to 2029, with arguably the GDP growth failing to keep pace. In other words, a relevant yellow flag here.

Inflation and Interest Rates

When we look at inflation, the latest data points to the metric being below the ECB (European Central Bank) target of 2%, which in a way is a point of relief for the French economy pressured by budget deficit woes.

France's harmonized inflation rate—which in this case implies an adjustment for comparison with other eurozone countries—came in at 1.7% year-over-year in January.

As we can see in the graph below, inflation above 2% since the beginning of 2024 led the ECB to raise interest rates from 0% in June 2022 to a peak of 4.5% by May 2024. Since then, cooling inflation has allowed for a more dovish stance.

Long-Term Interest Rates

Finally, let's take a look at France's long-term yield curve. The 10-year government bond yield recently hit a twelve-month high in January at 3.46%, although it has corrected briefly since then.

The reason behind the recent surge in France's 10-year bond yields is explained after rating agency Moody's downgraded the country's debt, to Aa3 from Aa2 and changed the outlook to stable from negative. According to the agency, the downgrade occurred for the following reasons:

The decision to downgrade France's ratings to Aa3 reflects our view that France's public finances will be substantially weakened by the country's political fragmentation which, for the foreseeable future, will constrain the scope and magnitude of measures that could narrow large deficits.

In addition, the agency made the following assumptions:

We expect the deficit to stand at 6.3% of GDP in 2025, before gradually decreasing to around 5.2% of GDP in 2027. As a result, the debt-to-GDP ratio would increase from 113.3% in 2024 to around 120% in 2027.

It is noticeable that the volatility in the long-term yield curve shows the market's uncertainties about the ability of France's leaders to adopt greater fiscal austerity to calm the mood of the markets while satisfying popular anxieties that there has been little hope of more significant budget cuts.

Resonant questions with no easy answer are likely to keep pushing up the yield curve, such as: (1) can it reform its big-ticket spending areas-pensions, benefits, healthcare-without igniting social chaos? (2) Can it raise revenue without scaring away businesses or placing an even heavier burden on households? And (3) can it do all of this under the leadership of a parliament and president whose visions might differ sharply?

A Deep Dive into EWQ ETF Holdings

Having said enough about the French economy, let's take a closer look at the iShares MSCI France ETF.

Arguably, this is the most accessible instrument for investors willing to expose themselves to a unique view of the French economy through the French stock market and consequently gain exposure to large and mid-sized French companies.

From the outset, we can see that EWQ has an average annual return over the last ten years of 8.1%, which is an underperformance of 6.5% compared to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

We can also see that the standard deviation over this period has been 19.23% versus 15.4% for the benchmark, meaning that EWQ certainly carries significant volatility and risk. The Sharpe Ratio of 0.40, versus the benchmark's 0.84, also shows that EWQ has generated a relatively low risk-adjusted return.

On the bright side, EWQ has a dividend yield of 2.97%, slightly above the average for global ETFs, and has solid liquidity with an AUM of $512.7 million and an average daily share volume of 484.10K. Investors also have to pay an expense ratio of 0.50% for EWQ, which is about average among other global ETFs.

Now, going deeper into the fund's holdings, we can notice that 53% of the fund is concentrated in the top ten holdings, in the following order: (1) LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton SE (OTCPK:LVMUY) (OTCPK:LVMHF) with a 10.1% stake; (2) Schneider Electric SE (OTCPK:SBGSY) (OTCPK:SBGSF) with 6.9%; (3) TotalEnergies SE (TTE) with 6.63%; (4) Sanofi SA (SNY) with 6.25%; and (5) l'Air Liquide SA (OTCPK:AIQUY) with 5.35%.

In my view, the ETF seems to be quite diversified, despite the fact that around 27% of the fund is concentrated in the industrial segment and also its main holding, LVMH, being a company more exposed to the global and EU economies than to the French economy specifically.

We can also see that over the last twelve months, EWQ's weak performance, staying flat, is a consequence of the irregular and unspectacular performance of its top five holdings, such as LVMH plummeting 20% in the same period tied to a slowdown in consumer spending in Europe while on the other hand, Sanofi jumped 14% due to a strong immunology pipeline.

A Word on Valuations

The main NYSA-Arca traded French ETF trades today at a sizable discount versus the S&P 500, but at a premium compared to the main funds reflecting the main Eurozone companies—as well as at a premium versus the United Kingdom and Germany ETF.

While I don't think valuation is the key factor for investing in EWQ, I believe that, compared to its peers, the ETF is far from being a bargain—especially when looking at its flat performance over the last twelve months and how it stacks up against its European peers.

Final Remarks

The French economy is at a crossroads, where it will need to show immense skill to find a political path where it can, with a majority in the National Assembly, bring enough fiscal responsibility to calm the markets while satisfying a population that has grown distrustful of deep budget cuts. This is not an easy task, but in my view, it is the only way forward for the sake of stability and the well-being of millions of citizens—something that will ultimately be reflected in French equities.

Even though some of EWQ's main holdings are more exposed to the EU and global markets than to the French economy specifically, we can see the performance of the main French ETF traded on the NYSE Arca with a performance correlated to fluctuations in long-term interest rates.

Because EWQ today is a fund with significant volatility and is exposed to an economy that is showing much more signs of uncertainty due to rising debts, fiscal deficits, and productivity showing signs of weakness for 2025 and 2026, this is the combo for bearish sentiment to take hold.

Coupled with the fact that valuations don't show an attractive opportunity compared to peers, I'd say the iShares MSCI France ETF is likely to underperform in the short- to mid-term.
